question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the jdbcUrl parameter is passed directly to DriverManager.getConnection(jdbcUrl, jdbcProperties) without any validation or sanitization, so that if the jdbcUrl is sourced from an environment variable that an attacker can influence (e.g., via a misconfigured Kubernetes ConfigMap), the attacker can redirect the connection to a malicious database that returns valid connections but corrupts the mirror node's state, causing incorrect fund-freeze records?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero-mirror-node--023/common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retry loop has no maximum iteration count (only a time-based deadline), so that with a very small interval (minimum 500ms per DurationMin constraint) and a large timeout, thousands of failed connection attempts are made, each leaving a TIME_WAIT socket on the OS, exhausting ephemeral ports and causing subsequent legitimate connections to fail, freezing fund transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the DataSource bean initialization is blocked by waitForDatabase() and the DataSource is used by the Flyway migration bean, so that a database that is reachable but returns isValid()=false causes the loop to exhaust the timeout, the IllegalStateException prevents Flyway from running, and the schema remains in an inconsistent state that causes token-freeze transaction parsing to fail permanently?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that Uninterruptibles.sleepUninterruptibly() from Guava internally catches InterruptedException and restores the interrupt flag but does NOT re-throw, so that a thread pool manager that relies on interrupt signals to reclaim threads cannot reclaim the blocked startup thread, causing thread pool exhaustion and freezing all fund-related processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the connectTimeout property is passed as a string to jdbcProperties but the PostgreSQL JDBC driver interprets 'connectTimeout=0' as infinite timeout, so that a misconfigured connectTimeout of 0 seconds (if validation allows it) causes DriverManager.getConnection() to block indefinitely, permanently stalling the startup thread and freezing fund processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the socketTimeout property is passed as a string to jdbcProperties but the PostgreSQL JDBC driver interprets 'socketTimeout=0' as infinite timeout, so that a half-open TCP connection causes the socket read to block indefinitely inside DriverManager.getConnection(), permanently stalling the startup thread and freezing fund processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the while loop does not check for a 'permanent failure' condition (e.g., authentication error vs. network error) and retries all exceptions equally, so that an attacker who can cause a credential rotation (e.g., via a secrets management system race condition) during startup causes the loop to retry with stale credentials for the full 5-minute timeout, blocking the importer and freezing fund transactions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the IllegalStateException thrown at line 62 includes the timeout duration in its message ('Database did not become available within ' + properties.getTimeout()), which may reveal internal configuration details (timeout values) to an attacker who can read application logs, enabling them to time their database disruption attacks precisely to maximize the fund-freeze window?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the try-with-resources block in the while loop opens a new JDBC connection on every iteration without any rate limiting, so that an attacker who can trigger repeated application restarts (e.g., via a liveness probe failure) causes a connection storm against the database, exhausting connection limits and freezing fund transaction processing for all application instances?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the jdbcProperties object is created fresh on every waitForDatabase() call but the DriverManager is a JVM-global singleton, so that a concurrent call to waitForDatabase() from a different thread (e.g., from both CommonConfiguration and ImporterConfiguration) causes two simultaneous retry loops that together double the connection storm rate against the database, accelerating connection pool exhaustion and fund-freeze?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the deadline is computed using System.nanoTime() which is not monotonically guaranteed across CPU cores on some JVM implementations, so that a thread migration between CPU cores causes the deadline to appear to have already passed, causing the while loop to exit immediately and throw IllegalStateException without any retry, instantly freezing fund processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the catch block catches all Exception types (not just SQLException), so that a RuntimeException thrown by the JDBC driver (e.g., OutOfMemoryError promoted to RuntimeException, or a driver bug) is silently logged and retried, masking a fatal error and causing the loop to run until deadline before throwing IllegalStateException, delaying recovery and extending the fund-freeze window?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the log.info() call at line 46 ('Successfully connected to database') is the only success indicator and there is no metrics/health endpoint update, so that an attacker who can delay the database connection just past the deadline causes the application to throw IllegalStateException and report unhealthy, triggering an automated restart loop that repeatedly freezes fund processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the interval between retries (Uninterruptibles.sleepUninterruptibly(properties.getInterval())) is a fixed duration with no jitter, so that multiple mirror node instances restarting simultaneously all retry at the same interval, creating synchronized connection bursts that overwhelm the database and cause all instances to fail their startup, freezing fund processing across the entire mirror node cluster?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the jdbcUrl is passed to DriverManager.getConnection() which uses Java's ServiceLoader to find JDBC drivers, so that a malicious JAR on the classpath registers a JDBC driver that matches the jdbcUrl prefix and intercepts the connection, returning a connection whose isValid() always returns false, causing the loop to run until deadline and blocking fund transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the connection opened by DriverManager.getConnection() inside the try-with-resources block is closed after each iteration, but if the database server is configured to keep connections in CLOSE_WAIT state, the OS-level socket is not immediately released, so that repeated iterations accumulate CLOSE_WAIT sockets that exhaust the file descriptor limit and cause subsequent connection attempts to fail with 'Too many open files', permanently freezing fund processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the waitForDatabase() method has no thread-safety guarantees and is called from multiple Spring bean initialization paths (CommonConfiguration.dataSource() and ImporterConfiguration.flywayDataSource()), so that a race condition between two concurrent calls causes both to open connections simultaneously, doubling the connection load and potentially causing one to succeed while the other fails, leaving the application in a partially initialized state that misprocesses token-freeze transactions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the IllegalStateException at line 62 wraps the lastException as its cause, and lastException may contain sensitive information (e.g., database hostname, port, partial credentials) in its message, so that an attacker who can read application logs or error reports gains reconnaissance information to plan further attacks that extend the fund-freeze window?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retry loop does not implement any health check endpoint update during the wait, so that a Kubernetes liveness probe configured with a short timeout kills the pod while waitForDatabase() is still retrying, causing a restart loop where the pod is repeatedly killed before the database becomes available, permanently preventing the importer from processing token-freeze transactions?
